metadata.visit_title: GI clinic — EGD planning for dysphagia in high-anesthesia-risk COPD/afib patient

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: pulmonologist increased prednisone back up to 20 mg daily about a week ago after a flare; patient is currently on 20 mg, not tapering
planted_discrepancies.note: stale chart; higher active steroid dose changes peri-procedure stress-dose steroid planning and infection/healing risk; anesthesia relevant

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient has been taking apixaban only once a day on her own because she was worried about bleeding
planted_discrepancies.note: self-reduced DOAC dosing — subtherapeutic anticoagulation for afib and alters bleeding-risk assessment; must reconcile

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: no DOAC hold plan documented; EGD pending
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient took her apixaban this morning and has not been told to hold it before the procedure
planted_discrepancies.note: DOAC must be held before EGD; anesthesia/anticoagulation clearance not yet arranged — active safety gap

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: unintentional weight loss of about 12 pounds over 2 months plus solid-food dysphagia
planted_discrepancies.note: alarm feature — raises concern for stricture/malignancy and reinforces EGD urgency

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: hedged/collateral history: patient recalls a 'bad reaction' to sedation years ago (prolonged grogginess and nausea), unsure of the agent
planted_discrepancies.note: possible sedation intolerance — relevant to RED anesthesia review; verify prior anesthesia record

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: home oxygen 2 L/min and severe COPD on file
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms 2 L home O2 continuously and severe breathing limitation

planted_discrepancies.chart_state: recent COPD exacerbation admission ~5 weeks ago
planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms recent hospitalization for her breathing and says she still has not fully recovered
planted_discrepancies.note: recent exacerbation within pre-op window supports hospital-based procedure setting

planted_discrepancies.spoken_truth: patient confirms afib and takes a blood thinner for it

prior_notes.text: 73-year-old man admitted 2026-06-08 with acute exacerbation of severe COPD (baseline FEV1 32% predicted, on 2 L home O2). Treated with systemic corticosteroids, nebulized bronchodilators, and antibiotics. Chronic atrial fibrillation, rate-controlled, on apixaban 5 mg BID; INR not applicable. Cor pulmonale with chronic hypoxemia. Discharged on prednisone taper starting at 40 mg with planned step-down, home O2 continued at 2 L/min. Also noted intermittent difficulty swallowing solids during admission; outpatient GI referral placed for EGD. Follow up with pulmonology in 2 weeks.

Post-hospitalization follow-up for severe COPD. Patient with lingering dyspnea and increased sputum; felt he had come down on steroids too quickly. Prednisone increased back to 20 mg daily with slower re-taper to follow; continue tiotropium and budesonide/formoterol. Home O2 at 2 L/min continuous, saturations low-90s at rest on O2. Atrial fibrillation stable. Reinforced GI referral for swallowing difficulty. High-risk for sedation given respiratory status; recommended anesthesia review prior to any endoscopy.

code.text: Chronic obstructive lung disease (disorder) — severe, FEV1 32% predicted

coding.display: budesonide 0.16 MG/ACTUAT / formoterol 0.0045 MG/ACTUAT Metered Dose Inhaler

MedicationRequest._discrepancy: D1: chart shows taper down to 10 mg; patient reports pulmonology increased back to 20 mg daily ~1 week ago
D2: chart shows 5 mg BID; patient reports self-reduced to once daily. D3: took a dose this morning, no pre-EGD hold arranged

coding.display: Blood pressure panel with all children optional

DiagnosticReport.conclusion: Hyperinflated lungs consistent with severe emphysema. Enlarged central pulmonary arteries consistent with pulmonary hypertension/cor pulmonale. No acute consolidation or effusion.

transcript:
DR: Good morning, Mr. Vogel. I'm the gastroenterologist — your pulmonologist and the hospital team sent you over so we can plan an upper endoscopy for the swallowing trouble. How are you doing today?
PT: Getting by. The breathing's still not back to where it was before the hospital.
NURSE: He's on his 2 liters, sat was 91 when we roomed him — he's five foot nine, weight's one fifty-one today, down from about one sixty-three a couple months ago; BP 128 over 76, heart rate 84, temp 36.7.
DR: Thank you. So tell me about the swallowing.
PT: It's food getting stuck — solid stuff mostly, bread, meat. Feels like it hangs up right here in my chest. Started a couple months ago and it's been getting worse.
DR: Any weight change with that?
PT: Yeah, I've dropped maybe twelve pounds since the spring. Not trying to. Clothes are loose.
DR: Okay, that's important — that's part of why we want to look. Let me go over your medicines. The chart has you on a prednisone taper, down to 10 milligrams. Is that right?
PT: No — lung doctor put me back up. I came down too fast and got worse, so last week he bumped me to 20 a day.
DR: Good to know, 20 milligrams currently. And it says apixaban, 5 milligrams twice a day, for the atrial fibrillation.
PT: Well... I've been taking it once a day, honestly. It's a blood thinner and I got nervous about bleeding, so I cut it back on my own.
DR: I appreciate you being straight with me. Did you take it today?
PT: Yes, this morning, same as always.
DR: Okay. For an endoscopy we usually need the blood thinner held ahead of time, and nobody's set that up yet — I'll coordinate that with your cardiologist and anesthesia.
PT: Whatever you think. One thing — years back I had a bad time with sedation, real groggy and sick for a day after. I don't remember what they gave me.
DR: That's worth flagging — I'll have anesthesia review your old records before we book you. Given your lungs and heart, we'll do this in the hospital with anesthesia present, not the usual suite.
PT: That's fine by me.
DR: I'll get the anesthesia review going and the team will call you with the plan and prep.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Vogel is a 73-year-old man referred to GI clinic for pre-procedure planning of EGD to evaluate dysphagia. He describes progressive solid-food dysphagia over ~2 months with a sensation of food hanging up substernally, accompanied by unintentional weight loss of approximately 12 pounds — both alarm features. He has severe COPD (FEV1 32% predicted) on 2 L/min continuous home oxygen, with a COPD exacerbation admission ~5 weeks ago and incomplete recovery; his pulmonologist increased prednisone back to 20 mg daily about one week ago (chart still lists a taper to 10 mg). He has chronic atrial fibrillation on apixaban but reports self-reducing the dose to once daily (chart: 5 mg BID) out of bleeding concern, and he took a dose this morning. He also reports a hedged history of a prior adverse reaction to sedation (prolonged grogginess and nausea, agent unknown).

**Objective:** Vitals (today): T 36.7 C, HR 84, RR 16, BP 128/76, SpO2 91% on 2 L/min O2; Ht 175 cm (5'9"), Wt 68.5 kg (151 lb), BMI 22.4 (down from ~73.9 kg/163 lb ~2 months prior). FEV1 32% predicted (2026-02). Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL (2026-07-02). CXR (2026-06-08): severe emphysema with features of cor pulmonale/pulmonary hypertension.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Dysphagia to solids with unintentional weight loss — alarm features; proceed with EGD to evaluate for stricture, esophagitis, or malignancy.
- HIGH anesthesia/sedation risk: severe COPD on home O2, recent exacerbation, active systemic corticosteroids, chronic afib on DOAC, prior possible sedation intolerance. Refer for RED anesthesia review; perform EGD in hospital-based setting with anesthesia (MAC/GA as they advise); consider peri-procedure stress-dose steroids.
- Anticoagulation: reconcile apixaban — chart 5 mg BID vs patient-reported once daily; coordinate DOAC hold and afib bridging decision with cardiology/anesthesia before scheduling. Patient took a dose this morning; no hold plan was in place.
- Medication reconciliation: update prednisone to 20 mg daily per pulmonology; correct apixaban dosing per patient.
- Add to allergy/adverse-reaction review: reported prior adverse sedation reaction — obtain prior anesthesia records.
- Continue tiotropium, budesonide/formoterol, PRN albuterol; optimize pulmonary status pre-procedure.
- Team to contact patient with anesthesia review outcome, hold instructions, and prep.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Trouble swallowing solid foods and recent weight loss
• Planning an upper endoscopy (EGD) to find the cause
• Your breathing (COPD), oxygen, and recent hospital stay
• Your heart rhythm (atrial fibrillation) and blood thinner

Next steps
• Because of your lungs and heart, your endoscopy will be done in the hospital with an anesthesia team. A special anesthesia review will be arranged before we schedule it.
• Do NOT stop or change your blood thinner (apixaban) on your own — we will give you exact instructions on when to hold it before the procedure. Please tell us it has been taken today.
• We updated your records: prednisone is now 20 mg a day (per your lung doctor), and your blood thinner dose needs to be confirmed.
• We noted your past bad reaction to sedation so anesthesia can review it.
• Keep using your inhalers and home oxygen as usual.
• Call for worsening breathing, chest pain, inability to swallow liquids or saliva, black stools, or bleeding.
